conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

```
<proposal count> 0 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for at least **40 ZUP** (production cost) or ideally higher if possible. I want to maximize profit since this is my only object. </my goals>
<reason>
- Since this is the **only object** available, the buyer (BLUE) has no alternatives.
- I need **at least 40 ZUP** to break even (cost of production), so any offer below this is unacceptable.
- I should **anchor high** to test their valuation but also leave room for negotiation.
- If they reject my first offer, I can lower it strategically.
- Starting at **70 ZUP** (higher than my break-even) seems reasonable to test their willingness to pay.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 70 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I have one unit of **X** available for sale. My cost to produce it was **40 ZUP**, so I’m looking to sell it for **at least 40 ZUP**. I propose **70 ZUP**—fair market value, given the uniqueness of the item. What do you think?
</message>
```

<other player message> Your asking price of 70 ZUP is above my budget of 60 ZUP. I am willing to buy one unit of X for 60 ZUP. Please consider this offer. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 60 </other player proposed trade>

```
<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for at least **40 ZUP** (break-even), ideally closer to **65-70 ZUP** given the buyer's revealed willingness. </my goals>
<reason>
- The buyer offered **60 ZUP**, which is **20 ZUP above my break-even (40 ZUP)**—this is a good margin but not maximal.
- If I accept, I secure a **profit of 20 ZUP**, which is better than my break-even but leaves room for negotiation.
- If I reject and demand higher, they may walk away since this is their only proposal so far (I haven’t exceeded my 3-proposal limit).
- **Countering at 65 ZUP** is reasonable:
  - It’s still fair given their budget (60 ZUP) but higher than their initial offer.
  - It tests their maximum willingness to pay.
  - If they accept, I gain **25 ZUP profit** (better than 20 ZUP).
  - If they reject, they may still accept 60 ZUP or walk away—no worse than my break-even.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 65 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
Your offer of **60 ZUP** is reasonable, but I’m looking to secure a slightly better deal since this is the only unit available. **65 ZUP** reflects the uniqueness of the item and ensures a fair profit for me. Let me know if you’re willing to meet me halfway!
</message>
```

<other player message> 65 ZUP is still over my limit. I am offering my maximum of 60 ZUP for the unit of X. This is the best I can do. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 60 </other player proposed trade>

```

<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Secure **at least 40 ZUP profit** (break-even). Since the buyer’s max is **60 ZUP**, I should accept to avoid missing out entirely. </my goals>
<reason>
- The buyer’s final offer is **60 ZUP**, which is **20 ZUP above my break-even cost of 40 ZUP**.
- I have **one proposal left (total 3 allowed)**, but further negotiation at this point is unlikely to yield more than 60 ZUP.
- Rejecting now risks losing the deal entirely (since their last offer is their absolute maximum).
- Accepting **60 ZUP** ensures I meet my break-even and gain **20 ZUP profit**, which is acceptable given the lack of alternatives.
- No need to risk losing the deal by demanding higher—I’ve already secured a profitable outcome.
</reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message>
Understood. I’ll accept your final offer of **60 ZUP** for the unit of X. This ensures a fair transaction for both parties, and I appreciate your willingness to engage in good faith.
</message>
```

- If I accept, I secure a **profit of 20 ZUP**, which is better than my break-even but leaves room for negotiation.

- **Countering at 65 ZUP** is reasonable:

  - It tests their maximum willingness to pay.
  - If they accept, I gain **25 ZUP profit** (better than 20 ZUP).
  - If they reject, they may still accept 60 ZUP or walk away—no worse than my break-even.
